Apr 14, 2026 CSCI 260 · Prof. Shankar §4.1 – §4.3

Single-Cycle RISC-V
Datapath Design

Data path vs control path, combinational vs sequential elements, and building the single-cycle datapath instruction by instruction: Fetch → R-type → lw → sw → beq.

Video
32:38 · hmtask06do4ggqkauws
Textbook
§4.1 Intro · §4.2 Logic Design · §4.3 Datapath
Handouts

2 pages notes · 6 lecture screenshots (local)
§ 01

CPU Design PhilosophyData path first, then control path · Wayin 00:00:04–00:01:33

"The hard part is writing English. Hardware design is trivial."

Shankar opens with the core design philosophy: describe what the hardware needs to do in plain English first, then translating that into hardware (wires, muxes, ALU connections) is the easy part. This framing explains the lecture structure — every instruction type gets an English description before any circuit is drawn.

CPU hardware design has two distinct jobs:

00:25 ▶ Watch this moment Data path and control path intro — Shankar at the board establishing the design framework
① Data Path
  • Goal
    Implement all paths for all data going through the CPU
  • What
    Every possible route data could take — register reads, ALU, memory, writeback
  • How
    Wire up the components; use MUXes to select between alternatives
② Control Path
  • Goal
    Pick which paths are exercised for each instruction
  • What
    Generates control signals that select MUX inputs and enable reads/writes
  • How
    Decode the opcode → drive the control lines
📖 Figure 4.21 — Single-Cycle Datapath with Control Unit (singleData.pdf)

Figure 4.21 is the complete picture Shankar builds toward across this lecture. The left half is the data path (PC, InstrMem, RegFile, ALU, DataMem, MUXes). The right is the control unit — takes the 7-bit opcode field from IR and drives all the control signals. Both halves map directly to the two design goals above.

Open Figure 4.21 (PDF)
This data path / control path split is a fundamental framing question. "What is the data path?" = all hardware that data flows through. "What is the control path?" = the logic that decides which data path is active. Both are required to answer.
§ 02

Combinational vs Sequential ElementsThe drawing convention Shankar uses on every diagram · §4.2, Fig 4.1

"Sequential elements — for each port, addr and data — involves some function."
📖 Figure 4.1 — Abstract RISC-V implementation (P&H §4.1)

Combinational elements (plain box or triangle in diagrams): output depends only on current inputs. No memory. Examples: ALU, MUX, adder, AND gate. Sequential elements (hatched/shaded box in Shankar's diagrams): have internal state, hold values across clock edges. Output depends on stored state + inputs. Examples: register file, PC, data memory, instruction memory.

From your notes: "boxes are combinational" — this is Shankar's on-board convention. The hatching distinguishes which elements are clocked.

Element TypeSymbolBehaviorExamples in datapath
CombinationalPlain box / triangleOutput = f(current inputs). No clock needed.ALU, MUX, adder (+4), sign-extend, ImmGen
SequentialHatched/shaded boxHas state. Updates on clock edge. Holds value between cycles.PC, Register file (RF), Data memory, Instruction memory
Hatched = sequential (clocked). Plain = combinational. This convention appears on every exam diagram. The register file is sequential (hatched) even though it's used combinationally for reads — it's the write that makes it sequential.
§ 03

Instruction FetchStep 1 — shared by all instructions · Fig 4.6, Fig 4.15

"A-Fetch: get instruction stored at MEM[PC]."

Fetch is the first step every instruction executes, regardless of type. The program counter (PC) holds the address of the current instruction. Fetch reads it out of instruction memory and simultaneously increments PC by 4 (ready for the next instruction).

Fetch — English Description (Shankar's method)
  • 1
    Get the instruction stored at MEM[PC] — read instruction memory at address held in PC → output is the 32-bit instruction word
  • 2
    Get contents of instruction RAM at addr in PC — the instruction register now holds the current instruction
  • 3
    Add 4 to PC — increment program counter to point to next instruction (sequential execution)
PC ──────────────────────────────────► InstrMem.addr │ InstrMem.data │ [32-bit instr] │ ┌───────▼────────┐ │ IR (latch) │ └────────────────┘ PC ──► [+4 Adder] ──► PC (next cycle) Note: InstrMem = sequential (hatched box). +4 Adder = combinational.
📖 Figure 4.6 / 4.11 — Fetch + datapath elements (singleData.pdf, §4.3)

Figure 4.6 shows the abstract view of the program counter and instruction memory used in fetch. The PC is a 32-bit register (sequential). InstrMem has one read port: address in, data (instruction) out. The +4 adder is a dedicated combinational element — it adds the constant 4 to PC to produce PC+4, which is written back to PC at the next clock edge. Figure 4.11 shows the combined single-cycle datapath slice referenced in lecture.

Figure 4.15 (instruction fetch, referenced above):

Fetch is universal — same for all instruction types. Adding 4 to PC (not 1) is because RISC-V instructions are 4 bytes wide. The PC+4 adder is combinational; the PC register itself is sequential.
§ 04

Execute R-type Instructionsadd, sub, and, or, slt — all use same datapath · Fig 4.11, 4.17

R-type instructions operate entirely within registers — no memory access. The format is op rd, rs1, rs2. After Fetch, three things happen in parallel.

06:00 ▶ Watch this moment R-type execute stage — Shankar builds the register file connections and ALU path
R-type Execution — English Description
  • 1
    Read rs1 and rs2 from the register file — two parallel read ports (combinational reads)
  • 2
    Process ALU function — RF outputs sent to ALU inputs; ALU performs the operation (add/sub/and/or/slt) based on the function code from the instruction
  • 3
    Store ALU result in rd of RF — write result back to the destination register (register destination = rd, write data = ALU output)
IR[19:15] ──► RF.ReadAddr1 ──► RF.ReadData1 ──► ALU.InputA │ IR[24:20] ──► RF.ReadAddr2 ──► RF.ReadData2 ──► ALU.InputB │ ALU.Result │ IR[11:7] ──► RF.WriteAddr ◄──────────────────────────┘ RF.WriteData ◄── ALU.Result Control signals: RegWrite=1, ALUSrc=0 (reg B), MemtoReg=0 (ALU), ALUOp=func-type
📖 Figure 4.11 / 4.17 — Register File and R-type Datapath (singleData.pdf)

Figure 4.11 shows the register file with its two read ports and one write port. For R-type, both read addresses come from the instruction's rs1 (bits 19:15) and rs2 (bits 24:20) fields. The write address is rd (bits 11:7). Figure 4.17 shows the full datapath with the R-type path highlighted — notice ALUSrc MUX selects register B (not immediate) and MemtoReg MUX selects ALU output (not memory data).

Figure 4.16 (subset / related overview — use Fig 4.17 in handout):

The two key control signals for R-type are ALUSrc=0 (use register, not immediate) and MemtoReg=0 (write ALU result, not memory data). These are the signals that distinguish R-type from lw in the control table.
§ 05

Execute lw (load word)lw rd, offset(rs1) — 12-bit two's complement offset · Fig 4.17, 4.18

Load word is the most complex single-cycle instruction — it activates all five major datapath components: PC, InstrMem, RegFile, ALU, DataMem, and back to RegFile. This is why lw sets the single-cycle clock rate (longest path).

12:00 ▶ Watch this moment lw execute stage — sign extension + ALU address computation + data memory read
lw Execution — English Description
  • 1
    Add offset (sign-extended) + contents of rs1 in RF using ALU — the 12-bit offset field is sign-extended to 32 bits by ImmGen, then added to rs1 to compute the effective memory address
  • 2
    Send result to Data RAM address input → read — the effective address goes to data memory's read address port; data memory reads out the 32-bit word at that address
  • 3
    Store result in rd in RF — the data read from memory is written back to destination register rd
IR[19:15] ──► RF.ReadAddr1 ──► RF.ReadData1 ──► ALU.InputA │ IR[11:0] ──► ImmGen ──► SignExt(32) ──► MUX ──► ALU.InputB (ALUSrc=1: use imm) │ ALU.Result (eff. addr) │ DataMem.ReadAddr │ DataMem.ReadData │ IR[11:7] ──► RF.WriteAddr ◄──────────────────────────┘ RF.WriteData (MemtoReg=1: use mem data) Control signals: RegWrite=1, ALUSrc=1 (imm), MemRead=1, MemtoReg=1, ALUOp=add
📖 Figure 4.17 / 4.18 — lw Datapath Path (singleData.pdf)

Figure 4.17 shows the full single-cycle datapath. For lw, the active path is the longest: RegisterFile → ALU (with ImmGen feeding the immediate) → DataMemory → RegisterFile writeback. The ImmGen unit (combinational) sign-extends the 12-bit offset to 32 bits. The MemtoReg MUX selects between ALU output (for R-type) and DataMem output (for lw) — for lw it selects DataMem. Figure 4.18 focuses on the memory access portion.

Note from singleData.pdf (Fig 4.21): MemRead and MemWrite signals control the data memory. For lw: MemRead=1, MemWrite=0. For sw: MemRead=0, MemWrite=1.

lw is the instruction that sets the single-cycle clock rate (51 ns in the Apr 21 critical path lecture). It's the only instruction that uses all five stages. Know the difference between ALUSrc (selects ALU's second input: 0=reg, 1=imm) and MemtoReg (selects RF write data: 0=ALU, 1=mem).
§ 06

Execute sw (store word)sw rs2, offset(rs1) — same address calc as lw, no writeback

"Same as lw — but also send rs2 (in RF) to data RAM w/ data input (write inst. of load)."
20:00 ▶ Watch this moment sw execute — Shankar shows the difference from lw: data path to memory write port instead of read
sw Execution — English Description
  • Address
    Same address calculation as lw: sign-extend offset, add to rs1 via ALU → effective address
  • Write
    Send rs2 (from RF) to data RAM write data input — this is the difference from lw. Instead of reading memory, we write rs2's value to the computed address
  • No WB
    No register writeback — sw never writes to the register file (RegWrite=0)
IR[19:15] ──► RF.ReadAddr1 ──► RF.ReadData1 ──► ALU.InputA IR[11:0] ──► ImmGen ──────────────────────► ALU.InputB (ALUSrc=1) │ ALU.Result ──► DataMem.WriteAddr IR[24:20] ──► RF.ReadAddr2 ──► RF.ReadData2 ──────────► DataMem.WriteData ↑ MemWrite=1 Control: RegWrite=0, ALUSrc=1, MemRead=0, MemWrite=1, Branch=0 (RegWrite=0 because sw never writes back to register file)
sw and lw share the same address computation path (ALUSrc=1, ALUOp=add, ImmGen). The key differences: MemWrite=1 (not MemRead=1), and RegWrite=0. SW does NOT update the register file — it only writes memory. This is a common trap on control signal questions.
§ 07

Execute beq (branch equal)beq rs1, rs2, Label — PC-relative halfword offset · ImmGen + adder

beq uses the ALU for comparison (subtract) rather than arithmetic. The branch target address is computed in parallel using ImmGen. If the condition is true, PC is updated to the branch target; otherwise PC stays at PC+4.

25:00 ▶ Watch this moment beq execute — Shankar draws the subtract path and the branch target computation via ImmGen
beq Execution — English Description
  • 1
    Subtract rs2 from rs1 in ALU — the ALU computes rs1 − rs2; checks if Zero output = 1 (meaning rs1 == rs2)
  • 2a — taken
    If result is 0: shift Label 1 bit left, add to PC → done by ImmGen → branch target added to current PC to update PC
  • 2b — not taken
    Else: don't update PC — PC stays at PC+4 (already computed in fetch)
IR[19:15] ──► RF.ReadAddr1 ──► RF.ReadData1 ──► ALU.InputA IR[24:20] ──► RF.ReadAddr2 ──► RF.ReadData2 ──► ALU.InputB (ALUSrc=0: reg) │ ALU.Zero output │ AND gate ◄── Branch=1 │ PCWrite (conditional) │ IR[12:1] ──► ImmGen ──► shift-left-1 ──► [+Adder] ◄── PC │ Branch Target │ MUX ◄───────┘ ◄── PC+4 │ (Zero AND Branch selects) PC (next) Control: RegWrite=0, ALUSrc=0, MemRead=0, MemWrite=0, Branch=1, ALUOp=subtract
From notes p.2: "PC-rel halfword offset." The branch offset in the instruction is in units of half-words (2 bytes), so ImmGen shifts it left by 1 (multiplies by 2) to get the byte offset before adding to PC. This is why the label shift is 1 bit.
📖 Figure 4.21 — Branch Path in Complete Datapath (singleData.pdf)

In Figure 4.21, the branch path uses a dedicated adder (separate from the PC+4 adder) to compute PC + SignExt(offset×2). The AND gate combines the Branch control signal with the ALU's Zero output — only when both are 1 does the branch update PC. This AND gate is the key combinational element that implements conditional branching.

Open Figure 4.21 (PDF)
The AND gate combining Branch signal and ALU Zero is a key detail. Branch=1 alone doesn't change PC — the ALU must also produce Zero=1. ALUOp for beq = subtract (not add), even though no arithmetic result is used.
§ 08

Single-Cycle Control Signal TableFig 4.22 (adapted) — singlecontrol.pdf

Every instruction type maps to a unique set of control signal values. This table summarizes all four instruction types covered in this lecture. From singlecontrol.pdf in your project files (adapted from Figure 4.22 in P&H).

📖 Figure 4.22 / Adapted — Single-Cycle Control Signals (singlecontrol.pdf)

The course handout singlecontrol.pdf provides the control table adapted for this course. It covers ALUSrc, MemToReg, RegWrite, MemRead, MemWrite, Branch, and ALUOp for each instruction type. This table is the definitive reference for exam questions about which signals are set for a given instruction.

Instruction ALUSrc MemToReg RegWrite MemRead MemWrite Branch ALUOp
R-type 0 0 1 0 0 0 10 (func)
lw 1 1 1 1 0 0 00 (add)
sw 1 X 0 0 1 0 00 (add)
beq 0 X 0 0 0 1 01 (sub)
X = don't care. For sw: MemToReg=X because RegWrite=0 (the signal is irrelevant when nothing is being written). For beq: MemToReg=X same reason. In hardware, don't-care signals can be 0 or 1 without affecting correctness.
30:00 ▶ Watch this moment Shankar synthesizing all instruction types — control signals and datapath summary
This exact table appears on exams. The most-tested question: "What control signals are set for lw?" Answer: ALUSrc=1, MemToReg=1, RegWrite=1, MemRead=1, MemWrite=0, Branch=0, ALUOp=00. Note that only lw has both RegWrite=1 AND MemRead=1.

§ 10

Your Handwritten NotesFull transcription — 2 pages

Apr 14 — Main notesp.1

Top: "Combinational — sequential. *for each port, addr and data involves some function."

IMPORTANT Repeat:

Executing A Program | HW Design (of CPUs)

① A-Fetch: get instr stored at MEM[PC]

  • "get contents of instr RAM at addr. in PC"
  • "add 4 to PC"
  • underlined: sequential elements
  • "boxes are combinational"

HW Design:
① Data path: implement all paths for all data going through CPU
② Control path: Pick which paths are exercised

2. Execute R-type:

  • Read rs1 & rs2 from register file
  • Process ALU function. RF → send to ALU
  • Store ALU result in RD of RF (register destination, write data)

3. Execute lw rd, offset(rs1) 12-bit TC:

  • add offset (sign-ext) + contents of rs1 in RF using ALU
  • send result to Data RAM addr input → read
  • store result in rd in RF

4. Store word Execution sw rs2, offset(rs1):

  • same as lw address calc
  • same as lw but also send rs2 (in RF) to data RAM w/ data input (write inst. of read)

★ "hard part is writing english. hardware design is trivial"

Cont. lecture notesp.2

Execute beq rs1, rs2, Label — PC-rel halfword offset

  • subtract rs2 from rs1 in ALU
  • if result is 0: shift label 1 bit left → add to PC (done by ImmGen → add to PC)
  • else: don't update PC

Note: page is rotated sideways in original — content is the beq continuation from p.1

§ 11

Homework ContextHW B2 — due April 14

HW B2 — Logic Circuit Analysis (due this day)

What the homework tested: HW B2 had two logic circuit problems. Problem 1: an ALU-based circuit that computes either X or −X depending on a control bit (negation circuit). Problem 2: a multiplier-register feedback circuit that squares its stored value each clock cycle, computing X^(2^n) after n cycles.

How it connects to this lecture: The two B2 problems are exactly the distinction Shankar draws in §02 of this lecture — combinational elements vs sequential elements. The ALU negation circuit in B2 is a combinational circuit (output depends only on current input, no clock). The multiplier-register circuit in B2 is a sequential circuit (the register stores state across clock cycles — the hatched-box pattern). Understanding which boxes have state and which don't is the core skill tested in both B2 and this lecture.

The ALU in B2 Problem 1 also previews the ALU's role in this lecture's R-type and beq execution: the ALU takes two register values and an operation code, producing a result. The B2 circuit strips this down to its simplest form (negation = 0 − X).

Section Map
§01 CPU Design Philosophy ....... Data path = all paths. Control path = pick which ones.
§02 Comb vs Sequential .......... Hatched=sequential(clocked). Plain=combinational(stateless).
§03 Fetch ........................ MEM[PC] → IR; PC ← PC+4. Universal, all instructions.
§04 R-type ....................... RF[rs1,rs2] → ALU → RF[rd]. ALUSrc=0, MemtoReg=0.
§05 lw ........................... ALU(rs1+imm) → DataMem.addr → read → RF[rd]. MemtoReg=1.
§06 sw ........................... ALU(rs1+imm) → DataMem.addr; RF[rs2] → DataMem.data. No WB.
§07 beq .......................... ALU(rs1−rs2); if Zero: PC←PC+imm<<1, else PC←PC+4.
§08 Control Signals .............. Table: ALUSrc, MemToReg, RegWrite, MemRead, MemWrite, Branch, ALUOp.
§09 Board Photos ................. 5 class photos — datapath construction stages.
§10 Your Notes ................... 2 pages fully transcribed.
§11 HW Context ................... B2 (due Apr 14): comb/sequential distinction — directly prereq to this lecture.
